content.body: A simple machine is a device that makes work easier by changing the size or direction of a force. The six simple machines are: lever, pulley, inclined plane, wheel & axle, wedge, and screw.

Mechanical Advantage (MA) = Load ÷ Effort
Velocity Ratio (VR) = Distance moved by effort ÷ Distance moved by load
Efficiency = (MA ÷ VR) × 100%

LEVERS have three parts: Fulcrum (pivot point), Load (force being moved), Effort (force applied).
Class 1: Fulcrum between effort and load (e.g. seesaw, scissors)
Class 2: Load between fulcrum and effort (e.g. wheelbarrow, bottle opener)
Class 3: Effort between fulcrum and load (e.g. tweezers, fishing rod)

content.title: Lever Calculation
content.body:
A crowbar (class 1 lever): effort arm = 90cm, load arm = 30cm.
VR = effort arm ÷ load arm = 90÷30 = 3
This means 1N effort can move 3N load (ignoring friction).
For levers: Effort × Effort arm = Load × Load arm (Principle of moments)

questions.question: Mechanical advantage is defined as:
questions.explanation: MA = Load/Effort. MA > 1 means effort is less than load — the machine helps you.

questions.question: In a Class 2 lever, the load is:
options: Between fulcrum and effort... wait: between fulcrum and effort
questions.explanation: Class 2: F—L—E. Load is between fulcrum and effort. Wheelbarrow is the classic example.

questions.question: A seesaw is an example of a class ___ lever.
questions.explanation: Class 1: fulcrum in the middle (F between L and E). Seesaw, scissors, crowbar.

questions.question: Effort = 50N, Load = 200N. Mechanical Advantage =
questions.explanation: MA = Load÷Effort = 200÷50 = 4

questions.question: Efficiency of a machine can never exceed:
questions.explanation: Due to friction, some energy is always lost. Efficiency < 100% in real machines.

questions.question: Effort arm = 120cm, load arm = 40cm. VR of lever =
questions.explanation: VR = effort arm ÷ load arm = 120÷40 = 3

questions.question: Tweezers are a class ___ lever.
questions.explanation: Class 3: effort between fulcrum and load (E—F—L from one end). Tweezers, fishing rod.

questions.question: A machine with MA = 5 and VR = 8 has efficiency:
A wheelbarrow is a class ___ lever.
questions.explanation: Wheelbarrow: wheel=fulcrum, load in bucket=between fulcrum and hands(effort). Class 2.

questions.question: Principle of moments states: Effort × effort arm =
questions.explanation: Sum of clockwise moments = Sum of anticlockwise moments about the fulcrum.

questions.question: A lever has effort arm 80cm, load 400N, effort 100N. Load arm is:
Which simple machine works on the same principle as a lever?
questions.explanation: Wheel and axle acts like a lever with wheel radius as effort arm and axle radius as load arm.

questions.question: Class 3 levers always have MA:
questions.explanation: In Class 3, effort is always closer to fulcrum than load, so you exert MORE force than load — MA < 1. They give speed advantage not force advantage.

questions.question: The fulcrum of a pair of scissors is the:
questions.explanation: The screw/rivet at the centre is the fulcrum (pivot point) of the scissors.

questions.question: VR of a machine is always:
options: Greater than or equal to MA
questions.answer: Greater than or equal to MA
questions.explanation: VR ≥ MA always. Efficiency = MA/VR ≤ 1 (100%).

questions.question: A crowbar lifts a 600N rock with 120N effort. MA =
If efficiency = 80% and VR = 5, then MA =
questions.explanation: Efficiency = MA/VR×100: 80 = MA/5×100 → MA = 4

questions.question: Which class of lever gives a mechanical advantage greater than 1?
questions.explanation: Class 1 and 2 can have effort arm > load arm, giving MA > 1. Class 3 always has MA < 1.

questions.question: The work input to a machine is always ___ the work output.
questions.explanation: Some input energy is always wasted as heat due to friction (unless machine is 100% efficient).

questions.question: A bottle opener is a class ___ lever.
questions.explanation: Bottle opener: fulcrum at bottle cap edge, load is the cap, effort at handle end. Class 2.

content.body: PULLEYS change the direction of a force and can reduce the effort needed to lift a load. The number of rope segments supporting the load equals the VR.

Pulley VR = number of rope segments supporting the load
Single fixed pulley: VR = 1 (only changes direction)
Single movable pulley: VR = 2
INCLINED PLANES (ramps): Make it easier to raise a load by spreading the work over a longer distance.

Inclined plane VR = Length of slope ÷ Height
Effort = Load × (Height ÷ Length) [ideal, no friction]

content.title: Pulley System
content.body:
A block and tackle with 4 rope segments supporting the load.
VR = 4. Load = 800N, efficiency = 75%.
MA = efficiency × VR / 100 = 0.75 × 4 = 3
Effort = Load ÷ MA = 800 ÷ 3 = 267N

content.title: Ramp
content.body:
A ramp is 5m long and 1m high.
VR = 5÷1 = 5
A 100N load needs ideal effort of 100÷5 = 20N to push up.

questions.question: A single fixed pulley has VR =
questions.explanation: A single fixed pulley only changes the direction of force; it doesn't reduce effort. VR = 1.

questions.question: A pulley system has 3 rope segments supporting the load. VR =
questions.explanation: For pulleys, VR = number of rope segments holding the load.

questions.question: An inclined plane is 8m long and 2m high. VR =
questions.explanation: VR = Length÷Height = 8÷2 = 4

questions.question: The advantage of an inclined plane is that it:
options: Reduces effort needed to raise a load
questions.answer: Reduces effort needed to raise a load
questions.explanation: A ramp spreads the work over greater distance, reducing force required at any one moment.

questions.question: A movable pulley attached to the load:
questions.explanation: A movable pulley has 2 rope segments supporting it, giving VR = 2 and halving the effort.

questions.question: Load = 600N, VR = 4, efficiency = 50%. Effort needed =
questions.explanation: MA = Eff×VR/100 = 0.5×4 = 2. Effort = Load/MA = 600/2 = 300N

questions.question: Ramp length 10m, height 2m. A 500N crate needs ideal effort of:
questions.explanation: Ideal effort = Load×height/length = 500×2/10 = 100N

questions.question: Which has greater VR: ramp (length 6m, height 1m) or pulley with 4 rope segments?
questions.explanation: Ramp VR=6/1=6. Pulley VR=4. Ramp has higher VR.

questions.question: Adding more pulleys to a block-and-tackle system:
questions.explanation: More pulleys = more rope segments supporting load = higher VR = less effort needed.

questions.question: A single fixed pulley is useful because:
options: It changes direction of force making work more convenient
questions.answer: It changes direction of force making work more convenient
questions.explanation: You can pull down instead of up — more comfortable, but no force advantage (VR=1).

questions.question: VR of block-and-tackle with 6 rope segments =
questions.explanation: VR = number of rope segments = 6

questions.question: Screw jack, car ramp, loading bay — these are all examples of:
questions.explanation: All use the principle of trading longer distance for less force — inclined plane principle.

questions.question: Effort = 150N, VR = 6, MA = 4.5. Efficiency =
questions.explanation: Efficiency = MA/VR × 100 = 4.5/6 × 100 = 75%

questions.question: If ramp height is increased but length stays the same, effort needed:
questions.explanation: VR = L/H. More height = smaller VR = less mechanical advantage = more effort.

questions.question: The work done by a machine:
options: Equals work input in ideal machine
Is always less than work input
Has nothing to do with work input
questions.answer: Equals work input in ideal machine
questions.explanation: Energy conservation: in ideal (frictionless) machine, work output = work input.

questions.question: A pulley system has VR=5, load=1000N, efficiency=80%. Effort =
The screw is a simple machine based on which principle?
options: Inclined plane wound around a cylinder
questions.answer: Inclined plane wound around a cylinder
questions.explanation: A screw is an inclined plane (ramp) wrapped into a spiral — each turn moves load a tiny height.

questions.question: A block-and-tackle with VR=3 lifts 300N with what ideal effort?
questions.explanation: Ideal (100% efficiency): Effort = Load/VR = 300/3 = 100N

questions.question: Friction in simple machines:
questions.explanation: Friction wastes energy as heat, so actual MA < VR and efficiency < 100%.

questions.question: Which machine type is used in a bicycle's chain drive?
questions.explanation: The pedals, chainring, and rear sprocket work as wheel-and-axle systems.
